Beginners, Amateurs, and Experts, 1st ed., 2018, https://web.getmonero.org/library/Zero-to-Monero-10-0.pdf.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Demographic information
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's basic demographic information, such as gender, age, date of birth, ethnicity, etc.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Biographical information
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's basic biographical information, such as their parents, where they were raised, main life events, relevant experiences, etc.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Personality profile
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's personality profile. The recommended model to be followed by the handler is the PEN model,which includes the traits of psychoticism, extraversion and neuroticism. The profiling of the source may be done by the collection of indirect indicators of its traits, obtained by the analysis of documents, questioning people who they interact with or by the observation of the source's behavior in social media and real life. Profiles are considered live documents, as they are to be updated by screeners and handlers. They can also be translated into other equivalent personality models, such as the Big Five model, depending on the needs of the operation and the abilities and knowledge of the handler and screeners.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Motivation profile
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's motivation profile, that is, whether they are more easily influenced by rewards or punishments, as well as their reaction to fight or flight situations. The motivational profile of the source depends on and correlates with their personality, so both should be taken into account together when screening a source. This subtechnique is based on Gray’s model79 80 81, which defines three motivational systems: the Behavioral Activation System (BAS), the Behavioral Inhibition System (BIS) and the FightFlight System. Handlers may identify if the source is more vulnerable to reinforcements due to having an overly activated BAS (typical in individuals with high neuroticism and extraversion) or if they are more vulnerable to punishments due to having a highly activated BIS (characteristic of introverted-neurotic people), as well as if their reaction to a dangerous situation would be to fight (such as low FFS and, therefore, high psychoticism individuals) or flight (more common in high FFS and low psychoticism individuals).

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Vulnerabilities
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's vulnerabilities in order to subsequently exploit them during the collection process. Although different ways to categorize vulnerabilities in human intelligence have been proposed, such as the MICES model (money, ideology, coercion, ego or sex), in this context a vulnerability should be understood in a wider range. Thus, a vulnerability can be anything that can be exploited by the handler in order to obtain information from a source, and it can be related to other factors detected in the screening process (relationships, social identity, values, personality…).

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Relationships and marital status
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's marital status, as well as other information about their relationships. This includes not just romantic or sexual relationships, but also platonic and any other kind of bond the source has.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Social identity and group affiliation
techniques.description: The handler may identify the source's social identity, an individual's self-concept that is derived from their membership in various social groups82. Therefore, the handler may identify the belonging and reference groups of the source, which include: ethnic background, religion, gender, sex, nationality, sexual orientation and any other group relevant to the source. Understanding the source's social identity will not only help the handler understand the groups they belong to, but also the ones they want to be a part of, as well as their attitude towards them. This can, in turn, help the handler tailor the approach and the subsequent persuasion techniques.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Political identity and affiliation
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's political identity, that is, the side of the political spectrum they are most aligned or they most agree with, as well as their affiliation or lack thereof to a political party.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Geographical profile
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's geographical profile, that is, the areas they tend to move around and spend most time in. This can give the handler insight into where the source lives, as well as about their personality traits, groups and activities.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Cultural factors
techniques.description: The handler may find out the cultural factors that influence the source's way of thinking and behaving. For instance, this may involve a review of how the source frequently posts about local festivals, religious events and national holidays. These posts include personal anecdotes, photos and even traditional recipes, indicating a strong cultural identity. Thus, the handler may notice that the source often shares content related to regional political movements and expresses pride in their cultural heritage.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Social abilities and assertiveness
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's level of social abilities and how assertive they are, as these are relevant factors when considering how easily manipulated a source can be.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: IQ
techniques.description:
The handler may find out the source's Intelligence Quotient (IQ), which can help tailor the approach techniques and collection process. Note that this technique explicitly refers to the IQ as the standardized score used to measure human intelligence and cognitive abilities relative Sociales 13, no. 2 (1974): 65–93, https://doi.org/10.1177/053901847401300204.

to a statistically normal distribution described by the American Psychology Association, not to the knowledge or intelligence value that a source may have within a given operation. The source’s IQ may also be considered when identifying the level of risk the source poses to the handler and the operation.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Sexual orientation and preferences
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's sexual orientation as well as their sexual preferences, including kinks and paraphilias, in order for them to be considered in the collection process, especially in approaches related with seducing the source.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Psychological pathologies
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's psychological health status and pathologies in order to consider them during the collection process. Note that the psychological health status may also be affected by the usage of certain legal and illegal drugs.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Health history and physical state
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's physical health history and pathologies, as well as their current physical state, in order to consider them during the collection process.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Educational level
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's educational level, which may be considered when designing and tailoring the collection process.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Emotional triggers
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's emotional triggers, that is, specific stimuli that generate certain emotions on the source. These triggers may be related to other factors included in the screening process and may be considered vulnerabilities. For instance, if the source frequently reacts strongly to discussions about economic hardships or social injustice, these topics can be identified as emotional triggers. The handler can then craft messages or engage in conversations that touch on these sensitive subjects to elicit emotional responses, building rapport and trust. This understanding of the source’s emotional landscape allows the handler to exploit these vulnerabilities, guiding the source towards revealing valuable information or taking specific actions aligned with the handler's objectives.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Languages
techniques.description: The handler may find out what languages the source speaks in order to tailor the approach and to better direct communication during the collection process. This should also include accents, local expressions and dialects amongst others.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Job history
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's current job and job history to better understand them, the areas they may be in and other factors, which can be later used to tailor the collection process, specially if the relationship transitions to the real world.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Most effective rewards
techniques.description: The handler may find out what the most effective rewards are for the source, which can be later used to maintain the relationship and the control of the handler over the source.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Most effective punishments
techniques.description: The handler may find out what the most effective punishments are for the source, which can be later used to maintain the relationship and prevent the source from revealing information to others, as well as to maintain the control of the handler over the source.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Military rank
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source's military rank, in case they are part of a military entity. This can be used to tailor the approach and collection process.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Skills and technical field
techniques.description: The handler may find out what the main skills of the source are, as well as their field of knowledge.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Economic status
techniques.description: The handler may find out what the source's economic status is, including how much money they earn, how much taxes they pay and their properties and investments.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Criminal records
techniques.description: The handler may find out if the source has a criminal record and its contents.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Perceptual filters (PALIO)
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source’s most pervasive perceptual filter. This technique involves identifying which type of content the source most commonly directs their attention to. The PALIO filter classifies these contents into people, actions, places, information or objects. Understanding the source’s primary perceptual filter can help in tailoring messages and interactions to match the source’s natural focus of attention.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Sensory filters (VAKO)
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source’s most predominant sensory filter. This involves identifying which sensory modality—visual, auditory, kinesthetic or olfactory—the source primarily uses to process information. Understanding the source’s sensory filter can aid in communicating more effectively by aligning with the source’s preferred mode of receiving information.

techniques.name: Profile the possible sources: Processing size
techniques.description: The handler may find out the source’s preferred processing size, that is, whether they tend to process information in big or small chunks. People who process information in big chunks may prefer a holistic view of situations, while those who process in small chunks may prefer detailed, step-by-step information. Profiling the source’s processing size can help in structuring communications in a way that the source finds most comprehensible and engaging.

techniques.name: Choose a source
techniques.description: The handler may choose a specific source in order to fulfill the operational requirements and informational needs depending on their willingness to cooperate/hostility, susceptibility to persuasion, access to the information or to other people of interest. They may also consider their possible collaboration motivations.

techniques.name: Choose a source: Assess willingness to cooperate/Hostility
techniques.description: The handler may evaluate the source's willingness to cooperate and degree of hostility. This can be done in multiple ways, by asking general questions to see if they answer or hesitate to do so, or by indirectly assessing this based on the profile's information, for example.

techniques.name: Choose a source: Access to the information
techniques.description: The handler may evaluate the source's current or potential access to the information needed to fulfill the collection requirements, including expected knowledgeability of the information targeted.

techniques.name: Choose a source: Accessibility to other people of interest
techniques.description: The handler may evaluate the source's current or potential access to the people of interest to the operation. This might mean using the initial source as an intermediate step necessary in order to approach another source. UT0302.004 Choose a source: Susceptibility to persuasion The handler may evaluate the source's level of susceptibility to persuasion. This should be assessed by considering the source's personality and motivation profile, susceptibility to incentives, either rewards or punishments, and general vulnerabilities, as well as their level of security training. UT0302.005 Choose a source: Determine collaboration motivations This TTP may include but it is not limited to identify economic reasons, ideology or affiliation, affiliation, sexual interest or any other motivation that may boost or mine the motivation to collaborate with the handler.

techniques.name: Identify source baseline
techniques.description: The handler may identify the source's communication baseline in order to later identify changes that may indicate a shift in the source’s mood, situation or veracity . In exclusively online relationships, baseline changes may also be an indicator of a change in the person behind a profile, if said profile is managed by more than one individual.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Writing style
techniques.description: The handler may identify changes in the source's usual online behaviors such as vocabulary, grammar and tone to notice any alterations that may indicate stress, deception risk or a change in the source's situation. This may also include any other kind of involuntary spelling mistake.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Posting times
techniques.description: The handler may track the typical times the source posts messages to identify recurrent patterns and any deviations that might indicate changes in the source's routine or location to assess reachability at different times and days of the week.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Devices
techniques.description: The handler may catalog the devices the source typically uses for communications to notice any unusual changes that could indicate security issues or changes in habits. This can be guessed from either metadata or information leaks.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Number of messages
techniques.description: The handler may count the average number of messages the source sends within a specific timeframe to identify any significant increase or decrease that could reflect changes in the source's engagement or activity level.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Average response times
techniques.description: The handler may pay attention or measure the average time the source takes to answer to messages, using this baseline to detect any noteworthy changes that could suggest a change in availability or interest.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Technical connection details
techniques.description: The handler may be capable, under some circumstances, of identifying technical connection details that may lead to detailed information about the source's location.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Facial gestures and body expression
techniques.description: The handler may identify the source's body language baseline in videos and video calls in order to be able to identify the possible deviations that may indicate risk of deception.

techniques.name: Identify baseline: Conversation length
techniques.description: The handler may identify the average length of the conversations with the source, either in voice calls or chats.

techniques.name: Technical exploitation for information
techniques.description: The handler may focus on extracting valuable information from sources using advanced technical means. This technique involves the strategic use of technology to monitor, intercept, and analyze communications and data from sources, often without their knowledge. It aims to gather actionable intelligence by leveraging technological vulnerabilities or the inherent characteristics of digital communications and storage.

techniques.name: Technical exploitation for information: White pixel tracking
techniques.description: The handler uses white pixel images embedded in emails or other digital communications. These nearly invisible images, when loaded by the recipient’s email client, send a request to a server that logs the time, IP address, and device details. This information can be used to confirm receipt of the message, track reader engagement and geolocate the device, providing valuable context and behavioral data about the source.

techniques.name: Technical exploitation for information: IP address identification using VoIP leaks
techniques.description: The handler may analyze network traffic moving to and from a source’s network so as to try to extract information from the data being transmitted, the protocols used, and the external and internal IP addresses involved. This can be useful when the communication involves Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) calls made by the source. By monitoring VoIP traffic, intelligence handlers can determine the source’s location and possibly their network environment.

techniques.name: Technical exploitation for information: Metadata leakage
techniques.description: The source may accidentally leak information from the metadata of the content and material shared by the source, including information from geo coordinates, devices used to take the pictures, and others. This information might be useful for the handler to assess the accuracy of the information and, ultimately, the credibility of the source as a whole.

techniques.name: Technical exploitation for information: Browser fingerprinting
techniques.description:
The handler may use browser fingerprinting to collect the unique combination of browser settings and extensions, device configurations and operating system details when a source visits a controlled webpage. This information can uniquely identify and track a source across sessions and websites, even if traditional cookies are blocked or cleared, offering persistent surveillance capabilities.

The handler and their team are trying to prepare, create and enrich the persona meant to interact with the source. This also includes the acquisition or development of the necessary technical capabilities and resources.

techniques.name: Create a false identity
techniques.description: The handler may create a new identity in order to conceal their own, which will be endowed with as much details as possible or needed. A false identity or persona does not necessarily mean a whole new individual designed by the intelligence agency or HUMINT team, as anything other than the real identity of the handler would be considered a fake identity. In this sense, changing the surname of the handler or equivalent changes can be considered a new identity that must be cultivated accordingly, taking into account if the operation it will be used in will be elicitation or collaboration. It is specially relevant in this technique to make sure that the selected traits, behaviors, groups, etc. of the persona are coherent and tailored to the specific source and operation.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Demographic information
techniques.description: The handler may use a previously designed or a newly created identity with specific demographic information, such as: gender, age, date of birth, ethnicity, etc.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Biographical information
techniques.description: The handler may use a previously designed or a newly created identity with a detailed biography and personal history, which may include information about: their parents, where they were raised, main life events, relevant experiences, etc.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Personality profile
techniques.description: The handler may use a previously designed or a newly created identity with a specific personality profile according to the personality model previously followed in the screening model. It must be noted that the selected handler or handlers must be capable of portraying the designed profile, including the display of the indicators of the selected traits. The personality profile must be coherent with the rest of information detailed in the false identity, including relationships, geographical profile, susceptibility to incentives, etc.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Motivation profile
techniques.description:
The handler may use a previously designed or a newly created identity with a specific motivational profile, that is, whether they are more easily influenced by rewards or punishments, as well as their reaction to fight or flight situations. The motivational profile of the fake persona depends on and correlates with their personality, so both should be taken into account together when designing a fake identity.

This subtechnique is based on Gray’s model83 84 85, which defines three motivational systems: the Behavioral Activation System (BAS), the Behavioral Inhibition System (BIS) and the FightFlight System. Handlers may identify if the source is more vulnerable to reinforcements due to having an overly activated BAS (typical in individuals with high neuroticism and extraversion) or if they are more vulnerable to punishments due to having a highly activated BIS (characteristic of introverted-neurotic people), as well as if their reaction to a dangerous situation would be to fight (such as low FFS and, therefore, high psychoticism individuals) or flight (more common in high FFS and low psychoticism individuals).

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Vulnerabilities
techniques.description: The handler may use a previously designed or a newly created identity with a specific set of vulnerabilities that must be correlated and coherent with the rest of the profile (relationships, social identity, values, personality, etc.). Vulnerabilities can be a useful tool to build rapport if used accordingly. In this context, a vulnerability is anything that could be exploited by an experienced individual in order to manipulate the persona.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Social identity and group affiliation
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their membership in groups. The handler may include information about the belonging and reference groups of the persona, which include: ethnic background, religion, gender, sex, nationality, sexual orientation and any other group of relevance to the operation.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Political identity and affiliation
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their political identity, that is, the side of the political spectrum they are most aligned or they most agree with, as well as their affiliation to a political party or lack thereof.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Geographical profile
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their geographical profile, that is, the areas they tend to move around and spend most time in.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Cultural factors
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including the cultural factors that influence the source's way of thinking and behaving.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Social abilities and assertiveness
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their level of social abilities and how assertive they are.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: IQ
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their IQ level.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Sexual orientation and preferences
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their sexual orientation (heterosexual, homosexual, bisexual, asexual or any other) as well as their sexual preferences, including kinks and paraphilias, in order for them to be considered in the planning of the strategy process, especially in approaches that include seducing the source.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Psychological pathologies
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their psychological health status and pathologies.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Health history
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their physical health history and pathologies, as well as their current physical state.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Educational level
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their educational level, which may be considered when designing and tailoring the collection process.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Emotional triggers
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their emotional triggers, that is, specific stimuli that generate certain emotions on the source. These triggers may be related to other factors included in the screening process and may be considered vulnerabilities.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Languages
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including the languages they speak.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Job history
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their job history or absence thereof.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Role and status
techniques.description: The handler may decide the relationship status the persona will have with the source, which may be posing as an equal, a superior or even an inferior.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Military rank
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their military rank, in case they are part of a military entity.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Skills and technical field
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their main skills, as well as their field of knowledge.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Economic status
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their economic status, including how much money they earn, how much taxes they pay and their properties and/or investments.

techniques.name: Create a false identity: Criminal records
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by including their criminal record and its contents.

techniques.name: Create profiles
techniques.description: The handler may create different social media or equivalent profiles in order to make the fake persona more real and/or to communicate with the source. The content of the created profiles must be coherent with the contents of the identity and must be kept alive, that is, taken care of during the whole of the operation or as long as it is needed by interacting from time to time. The newly created profiles may be associated with a fake identity (a non-existent persona), the real handler (but with less details or altered information) or a third person unaware of the usage of their identity for this operation. However, impersonation entails legal consequences that the handler must take into account before carrying it out.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Email accounts
techniques.description: The handler may create email accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: General social media
techniques.description: The handler may create general social network accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source. This includes Twitter, Instagram, TikTok and other mainstream social media.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Professional social media
techniques.description: The handler may create professional social networks accounts, such as LinkedIn, to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Forums
techniques.description: The handler may create accounts in forums to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Virtual reality avatars
techniques.description: The handler may create virtual reality accounts and avatars to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Technical social media
techniques.description: The handler may create technical social networks accounts, such as GitHub, to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Dating apps
techniques.description: The handler may create dating apps accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source. This can be specially useful in approaches that use honey traps or seduction techniques.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Messaging apps
techniques.description: The handler may create messaging apps accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Videogame communities
techniques.description: The handler may create accounts in videogame communities to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Create profiles: Other
techniques.description: The handler may create accounts in other kinds of social media or communities to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles
techniques.description: The handler may acquire different social media or equivalent profiles in order to make the fake persona more real and/or to communicate with the source. This does not only reduce the need for enrichment (though it does not eliminate it), but also it may provide followers and relationships. The difference with “UT0402 - Create profiles” is that this technique implies the acquisition (potentially, paying for them) of profiles. The newly acquired profiles may represent a fake identity (a non-existent persona) or a third person unaware of the usage of their identity for this operation. However, impersonation entails legal consequences that the handler must take into account before carrying it out.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Email accounts
techniques.description: The handler may acquire email accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: General social media
techniques.description: The handler may acquire social media accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Professional social media
techniques.description: The handler may acquire professional social media accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Forums
techniques.description: The handler may acquire specific forum accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Virtual reality avatars
techniques.description: The handler may acquire virtual reality avatars to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Technical social media
techniques.description: The handler may acquire technical social media, such as GitHub. accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Dating apps
techniques.description: The handler may acquire dating apps accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Messaging apps
techniques.description: The handler may acquire messaging apps accounts to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Videogame communities
techniques.description: The handler may acquire accounts in videogame communities to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Acquire profiles: Other
techniques.description: The handler may acquire accounts in other kinds of social media or communities to back up the fake identity and to communicate with the source.

techniques.name: Compromise real profiles
techniques.description: The handler may compromise profiles in order to gain access to information from parties somewhat related with the source. Compromise can take place using several methods that may include (but are not limited to) phishing, spear phishing, vulnerabilities exploitation, watering-hole attacks amongst others. This action may have serious legal and ethical implications, as well as major reputational implications in case of being discovered.

techniques.name: Compromise real profiles: Source
techniques.description: The handler may compromise the real profiles of the source, such as social media accounts, email accounts, or other online identities, in order to gain access to information directly related to the source. This may involve techniques such as phishing, spear phishing, or social engineering attacks targeted specifically at the source. By gaining control over the source's online presence, the handler can gather valuable intelligence and monitor the source's activities without their knowledge.

techniques.name: Compromise real profiles: Source’s environment
techniques.description: The handler may compromise the real profiles of the source's environment, including individuals, organizations, or entities closely associated with the source. This could involve targeting friends, family members, colleagues, or employers of the source in order to gain access to information indirectly related to the source. By infiltrating the source's social circle or professional network, the handler can gather additional intelligence and leverage these connections to manipulate or influence the source.

techniques.name: Compromise real profiles: Other third parties
techniques.description: The handler may compromise the real profiles of other third parties who are not in the source’s standard circle. This could include compromising the profiles of individuals or entities that the source communicates with regularly, such as administrators of known platforms the source uses or any other individual or entity that may have visibility of the source’s profile. .

techniques.name: Create content
techniques.description: The handler may create a variety of multimedia contents related with the fake persona in order to make it appear more real. These contents can be either created by AI or by any other tool, and must be coherent with the contents of the identity.

techniques.name: Create content: Images
techniques.description: The handler may create images to back up the fake identity. This can include editing existing pictures or generating them via AI for social media profile pictures or similar.

techniques.name: Create content: Text-based content
techniques.description: The handler may create text-based content to back up the fake identity. This can include writing done by the handler or generating AI text for social media profile descriptions, communication with the source, or others.

techniques.name: Create content: Audio
techniques.description: The handler may create audio content to back up the fake identity, either by AI or any other means.

techniques.name: Create content: Video
techniques.description: The handler may create video content to back up the fake identity, either by AI or any other means.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity with relationships, whether real or fake. These relationships can be of different kinds and not just romantic or platonic. The created relationships may be with real or fake people. It must be noted that the fake relationships that are created from nowhere may need to be enriched as well, which can, in turn, require more resources.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships: Family ties
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by creating a family tree or disperse familiar ties.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships: Professional ties
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by creating professional ties with work colleagues.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships: Group ties
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by creating ties with specific individuals from target groups.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships: Romantic and sexual ties
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by creating bonds of sexual, romantic or marital nature.

techniques.name: Create fake relationships: Enemies
techniques.description: The handler may enrich a previously designed or a newly created identity by creating adversarial ties with other individuals or groups.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline
techniques.description: The handler may develop a baseline for the fake persona's interactions with the source which must be consistent with the selected profile.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline: Writing style
techniques.description:
The handler may carefully craft the writing style used in interactions with the source to develop a consistent baseline. This involves considering factors such as tone, vocabulary, grammar, and level of formality to ensure that the fake persona's communications remain consistent with the selected profile. By maintaining a consistent writing style, the handler can reinforce the credibility of the persona and minimize the risk of arousing suspicion from the source. When developing the writing style of the fake persona, the handler may consider the following factors and tailor them to the source:

Formality level. Determine the appropriate level of formality based on the nature of the relationship with the source and the context of the communication.

Tone. Choose a tone that aligns with the desired perception of the fake persona, whether it be friendly, professional, authoritative, submissive or casual.

Vocabulary. Select vocabulary that reflects the persona's background, education level, and cultural context, ensuring that it is consistent with the selected profile.

Grammar. Pay attention to grammatical conventions and writing standards to maintain coherence and clarity in the communication.

Sentence structure. Craft sentences that are easy to understand and in line with the persona's presumed linguistic capabilities and preferences.

Length of messages. Determine an appropriate length for messages to balance conveying necessary information with maintaining the reader's interest and attention.

Use of emoticons or emojis. Decide whether the use of emoticons or emojis is appropriate based on the persona's personality and the nature of the relationship with the source, as well as the source’s profile and behavior.

Frequency of greetings and farewells. Establish a consistent pattern for greetings and farewells to maintain continuity and familiarity in the interaction.

Consistency in formatting. Maintain consistency in formatting elements such as font style, size and color to create a cohesive visual impression.

Personalization. Incorporate personal touches or details into the communication that align with the persona's backstory or interests, enhancing authenticity and rapportbuilding with the source.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline: Posting times
techniques.description: The handler may establish a baseline for the posting times of the fake persona's interactions with the source. This way, the handler can establish a pattern that may give information about the job, habits or time zone of the fake persona. This technique also involves determining optimal times for communication based on the source's habits and preferences, as well as external factors such as time zone differences. By consistently posting at specific times, the handler can create a predictable pattern of interaction that reinforces the authenticity of the persona and facilitates the ongoing engagement with the source.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline: Devices
techniques.description: The handler may designate specific devices for communication with the source to develop a consistent baseline. This involves using consistent hardware (e. g., smartphones, laptops) and software (e. g., messaging apps, email clients) to ensure that all interactions maintain a uniform appearance and digital footprint. By limiting communication to designated devices, the handler can mitigate the risk of inadvertently revealing inconsistencies in the fake persona's identity or behavior. In this technique, the handler may opt to expose the type of device using. This can be intentionally done by deliberately exposing the device or application used to answer a message. For example, some applications add some metadata to the message announcing the name of the application used to publish the message such as “published with Tweetdeck” or “Sent from Android”. Alternatively, the use of a desktop app instead of a mobile app can be inferred from the types of messages shared. For example, mobile applications, when they have the spelling checking activated tend to start each message with a capitalized word. This is not the typical case when writing using a desktop application, where the user will have to deliberately use the capitalized letter exposing for an expert eye the fact that the conversation is not taking place from a mobile phone with the implications that this may have to maintain the alibi of the handler.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline: Number of messages
techniques.description: The handler may establish a baseline for the frequency and volume of messages exchanged with the source. This involves monitoring and controlling the rate at which messages are sent and received to maintain a consistent level of engagement over time, as well as the length of the message and the latence, that is, the time that it takes the fake persona to write a new message. By adhering to a predetermined number of messages per interaction session or per day, the handler can regulate the flow of communication and avoid raising suspicions about the authenticity of the persona or the nature of the relationship with the source.

techniques.name: Develop a baseline: Adapt baseline
techniques.description: The handler may periodically review and adapt the established baseline to account for changes in circumstances or objectives. This involves monitoring the effectiveness of the baseline in achieving strategic goals and making adjustments as necessary to optimize performance. By remaining flexible and responsive to evolving dynamics, the handler can ensure that the fake persona's interactions with the source remain effective and aligned with operational objectives.

techniques.name: Conceal identity
techniques.description: The handler may use different tools and procedures in order to ensure their real identity, as well as their association with the intelligence agency, are concealed and unknown to the victim.

techniques.name: Conceal identity: Safe networks
techniques.description: The handler may use safe networks to conceal their identity and activities from detection. This entails operating within secure network environments that are resistant to surveillance and monitoring. By leveraging virtual private networks (VPNs), proxies, secure servers, and other network security measures, the handler can maintain anonymity and protect the confidentiality of their interactions with the source.

techniques.name: Conceal identity: Safe communication protocols
techniques.description: The handler may employ safe communication protocols to conceal their identity and maintain operational security. This involves using encrypted messaging platforms (such as Bitmessage or Tox), anonymous email services or email services with no or low know your customer (KYC) policies, and other secure communication channels to exchange information with the source. By adhering to strict communication protocols and best practices, the handler can mitigate the risk of exposure and ensure that their true identity remains undisclosed.

techniques.name: Conceal identity: Hide communication
techniques.description: The handler may find it necessary to hide the mere existence of a communication itself under certain circumstances, even when participants' identities and confidentiality are guaranteed. In such cases, handlers should identify cover channels and employ steganographic tools to conceal the messages being exchanged, including encrypted ones, to avoid detection. Steganography can be applied to a variety of cover channels, such as images, videos or even text. By embedding messages within these media types, handlers can ensure that the communication remains undetected by adversaries or monitoring systems, thereby enhancing the operational security of the HUMINT operation.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach
techniques.description: The handler may prepare the first approach to the source in advance in order to ensure every aspect that can be controlled is taken into consideration and adequately tailored to the source.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach: Develop a pretext
techniques.description: The handler develops a carefully constructed scenario or role that serves as a cover for their true intentions. This could involve posing as a journalist, researcher, developer, affiliate, or any other plausible identity that facilitates engagement without raising suspicions. The pretext approach allows the handler to gather information under the guise of legitimate activities, making it easier to access sensitive or guarded information without alerting the source. The chosen pretext must be coherent with the control, obtaining trust and persuasion techniques that will subsequently be used with the source.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach: Select primary approach techniques
techniques.description: The handler may identify what approach technique or combination of techniques can be more effective on the source and, therefore, should be selected over others.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach: Select alternate approach techniques
techniques.description: The handler may identify what approach technique or combination of techniques could also be effective on the selected source.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach: Select recording and reporting method
techniques.description: The handler may identify and select one or multiple recording and reporting methods that will be used during the operation. This involves choosing the most appropriate technology or method to record the interaction with the source. This could include audio recording, video recording or written notes. The choice depends on factors such as the sensitivity of the information being discussed, the preferences of the source and the resources available. If dealing with sensitive information or sources in potentially hostile environments, encryption and secure storage methods may be necessary to protect the integrity and confidentiality of the recorded data.

techniques.name: Prepare the approach: Select topics to be covered
techniques.description:
The handler may identify and prepare the main topics to be covered beforehand. This does not only include those topics related to the intelligence collection requirements, but also other issues that should be understood by the handler in order to have a normal conversation with that specific source.

The handler is trying to influence the source in order to get them to collaborate. This process includes obtaining and maintaining initial trust and rapport, as well as persuading the source by changing their attitude towards the idea of talking with them. The handler’s goal during this tactic is to exert psychological influence on the source in order to subsequently carry out Control and Impact techniques. It is advised to perform these actions without the source knowing they are being subjected to them, as this knowledge can generate reactance against the handler if the source feels they are being manipulated. Moreover, some of these techniques, specially those requiring knowledge of human psychology concepts and theories like the Elaboration Likelihood Model 86 of persuasion and personality theories, should be either carried out by an experienced handler with said knowledge or by an handler advised by an expert in these topics in order to be applied properly. It must be noted that, although building rapport is a necessary step prior to obtaining information, and should be specially considered during the first approaches, the techniques from this tactic should not be disregarded once initial trust is established, but rather should be considered during the whole of the relationship.

techniques.name: Tailor the message
techniques.description: The handler may design or adapt the messages given to the source so that they match with their characteristics, which can be used to enhance persuasion and trust. This technique relies on the persuasion technique of tailoring, a specific type of matching which relates the characteristics of the receptor (the source, in HUMINT contexts) to the variables used in the persuasive message in order to enhance persuasion87 88. In this sense, the personality of the source, as well as other psychosocial, perceptual and cognitive characteristics will be taken into account when communicating with the source.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Perceptual filters (PALIO)
techniques.description:
The handler may tailor their messages to the source’s most pervasive perceptual filter. This technique is based on the PALIO filter, a classification of the contents towards which a source directs their attention the most89. In this sense, the handler will primarily focus the content of their messages on one or more of the following contents, depending on where the source's attention tends to focus: people, actions, places, information or objects. This will, in turn, make the source feel more familiar and, therefore, comfortable with the handler. For example, if a source tends to talk about the people they are with and the places they visit, in order to make the source focus on a certain piece of information they want them to remember, they could emphasize how nice the receptionist was or what a view a hotel had.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Sensory filters (VAKO)
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to the source's most pervasive sensory filter. This technique is based on the VAKO filter, a classification of the most predominant sensorial register used by the source, which also may be the most easy to remember90. In this sense, the handler will primarily focus the content of their messages on one or more of the following contents: visual, auditive, kinesthetic and olfactory. This will, in turn, make the source feel more familiar and, therefore, comfortable with the handler. For example, if a handler wants a source whose predominant sensory filter is auditory, they could prefer to call the source or send audio messages, as well as talking about their music taste or sharing songs.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Processing size
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to the source's processing size, that is, whether they process information in big or small chunks. People who tend to process big chunks of information may be approached with a more holistic point of view, describing the whole of a situation, while people who tend to process information on smaller chunks should be approached with a detailed description of a situation91. If the designated source tends to have a more holistic way of seeing the world, the handler can send long, extensive text messages and deep reflections, whereas if the source processes information in small chunks, they could explain in detail a specific topic of information.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Need for closure
techniques.description:
The handler may tailor their messages to the source's need for closure, the psychological concept that describes the need to find definite answers and avoiding ambivalence 92 by providing conclusive responses and avoiding uncertainty in their conversations. This may enhance trust and persuasion. In particular, individuals with a low level of need for closure tend to be more vulnerable to messages that include certain persuasive variables, such as message repetition 93. On the other hand, sources with a high level of this need will be more easily persuaded if the message is communicated by an expert, especially when the source is on high elaboration 94, that is, when they have the capacity and motivation to think a lot about the topic. inteligencia económica y relaciones internacionales, no. 1 (2019): 1. inteligencia económica y relaciones internacionales, no. 1 (2019): 1. Social Psychology, Vol. 1 (Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage Publications Ltd, 2012), 224–45, https://doi.org/10.4135/9781446249215.n12. Actitud Que Me Benefician?’, Journal of Economic & Business Intelligence , no. 1 (Incluye la presentación de la revista por parte del Editor-jefe Javier Oubiña Barbolla (2019): 35–41.

For example, if a source with a high need for closure is in an ambiguous situation, the handler may try to persuade them by giving them a definite answer and repeating it multiple times, making sure the message is clear and understood by the source. However, if the source has a low need for closure and, therefore, does not really care about definite answers, the handler can simply reference their expertise in the matter to persuade them.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Need for cognition
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to the source's need for cognition, the level to which a person thinks they enjoy thinking about a topic95, by presenting an idea or topic as attractive to people who take pleasure in pondering in order to make them choose that idea or topic, guiding them and giving them an intrinsic reward by satisfying said need96. For example, if a source has a clearly high need for cognition, the handler may mention how a certain topic they want the source to be interested in really made them ponder about something or how much they enjoyed learning about it.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Emotions
techniques.description:
The handler may tailor their messages to the source's emotional valence, either positive or neutral emotions, or to a specific emotion 97. Therefore, by using messages that refer to the same emotion, or just the same valence, as the source's at that particular moment may enhance trust in the handler.

For example, in chat conversations, this might be done by adapting the frequency and variety of emoticons, gifs, stickers and memes to the ones the source uses during their communication. In this way, if a source seems to have a positive emotion, the handler can react with text messages or multimedia, such as emoticons, that display a positive emotion, or even the same as the source’s.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Arousal
techniques.description:
The handler may tailor their messages to the source's arousal, which in general terms refers to the state of physiological and psychological activation or readiness in an individual (although there are multiple definitions for this concept98), to increase persuasion99. Therefore, by using Psychology 42, no. 1 (1982): 116–31, https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.42.1.116. Perceived Message Quality in Gainand Loss-Frame Antidrug Persuasion of Adolescents’, Health Communication 25, no. 4 (28 May 2010): 303–11, https://doi.org/10.1080/10410231003773326; Duane T Wegener, Richard E. messages that reflect the same intensity or arousal as the source's at that particular moment may enhance trust in the handler. For example, if the source seems quite agitated, the handler can display an equally excited mood instead of a bored or relaxed mood to increase persuasion. This can be shown by both verbal communication (text or audio messages) and multimedia (pictures, emoticons, gifs, etc).

techniques.name: Tailoring: Social desirability
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to the source's self monitoring level, that is, the level of adaptation of one's behavior to the social environment, with the objective of not being judged100. In particular, individuals with a high level of self monitoring tend to be more easily persuaded when the message highlights the social benefits of accepting a proposal or when it references the social validation principle, the tendency of individuals to conform to the actions or opinions of others in order to validate their own behavior or beliefs101. For example, if trying to persuade the individual to provide information, the handler could highlight how others in similar positions have already shared their insights, framing this behavior as socially desirable and widely accepted. By showcasing the social approval and potential benefits, such as increased respect and recognition within the community, the handler leverages the individual's tendency to adapt behavior for social acceptance, thereby increasing the likelihood of compliance.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Social identity
techniques.description:
The handler may tailor their messages to align with the source's social identity or group affiliation. By incorporating language, values or beliefs associated with the groups the source identifies with, the handler can enhance trust and rapport. This technique relies on the understanding of social psychology and group dynamics to effectively resonate with the source's sense of belonging and identity. In an online HUMINT context, a handler could apply this technique by engaging with a source who is an active member of a specific online community, such as a veterans' forum. In this case, by tailoring their messages to include military jargon, expressing shared values like loyalty and honor and referencing common experiences from, for example, the military service, the handler may align their communication with the source's social identity as a veteran. In the case of a technical community, a handler may be able to get identified with the source quoting past experiences and successes which may be useful to cultivate a deeper social link. 98

Matching Effects in Persuasion’, Journal of Consumer Psychology 31, no. 2 (2021): 382–414, https://doi.org/10.1002/jcpy.1198.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Morality
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to appeal to the source's moral values and principles. By framing information or requests in a way that aligns with the source's moral compass, the handler can foster a sense of moral alignment and trust. For example, if, by researching the source’s social media activity, the handler identifies the source's strong advocacy for climate change action, they can emphasize how sharing specific information could help combat environmental degradation, presenting the request as a moral duty to protect the planet.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Political affiliation
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to resonate with the source's political affiliation or ideological stance. By addressing issues or topics in a manner consistent with the source's political beliefs, the handler can establish common ground and credibility. For example, if the source is known to have conservative views, the handler could discuss topics like national security, economic policies or patriotism in a way that resonates with conservative values.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Cultural bases
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to reflect the cultural norms, values, and customs of the source's cultural background. By demonstrating cultural sensitivity and awareness, the handler can build trust and rapport with the source. If a handler is working to gather intelligence from a source in a foreign country, they might tailor their messages to align with the source's cultural norms and values. For example, if the source is from a culture that places high importance on respect and hierarchy, the handler would use formal language, honorifics and acknowledge the source's expertise or status in their communications. Additionally, the handler might reference local customs, holidays or historical events to show a deep understanding and respect for the source's background. This cultural sensitivity helps to build trust and rapport, making the source more likely to share valuable information.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Personality profile
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages based on the source's personality profile. By considering factors such as extroversion, psychoticism and neuroticism, the handler can customize their approach to resonate with the source's unique personality traits. This technique involves a previous screening of the personality and motivational profile of the source. For example, if during a chat conversation the handler is communicating with an extraverted source who tends to use a high volume of individual messages instead of one big message containing all the information, the handler will answer accordingly, answering to each message individually and conversing by using a similar number of messages to the source. Another example would be to have a neurotic source who tends to use highly emotional messages instead of rational ones, the handler's response may be adapted to match their style by using words referring to emotions, or sending emoticons and other multimedia content that references their or the source’s moods. Another example would be to talk with a source using verbal and non-verbal behaviors that resemble their own profile. This could include talking about how “cool” a party was and how many new people the handler met when speaking with an extraverted source, mentioning that they had previously dyed their hair purple or speaking about crime podcasts with a source with high psychoticism, or discussing anxiety-inducing stimuli and anxiety-reducing techniques with a neurotic individual. Although tailoring the message to the profile, that is, the combination of the three traits, and not just one trait would be the most effective way to apply this technique, it may not always be possible or worth the resources it requires. Therefore, tailoring may focus only on the dominant trait of the source. It must be noted that each trait and profile is more or less vulnerable to certain persuasive techniques and variables so, when applying this technique, other UTA05 Influence techniques should be considered and applied jointly.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Present rational arguments
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to present rational arguments and logical reasoning that appeal to the source's analytical thinking. Some personality profiles might be more easily persuaded by rational arguments (low neuroticism individuals102, for example), although vulnerability to these kinds of arguments will also depend on if the attitude we are trying to change is based on emotion or cognition. Rational arguments seem to be as persuasive as emotional arguments when the attitude has a cognitive base, but they do not seem to be as persuasive on emotionally-based attitudes103.

techniques.name: Tailoring: Present emotional arguments
techniques.description: The handler may tailor their messages to evoke emotional responses that resonate with the source's feelings and sentiments. By appealing to the source's emotions, such as empathy, compassion, or excitement, the handler can establish a deeper connection and foster trust. This technique involves understanding the emotional triggers and motivations of the source to effectively influence their behavior. This technique is different from UT0501.006 - Tailoring: Emotions in the fact that this technique (UT0501.015) does not refer to mirroring the exact valence or emotion as the handler, but rather to the general idea of using persuasive arguments that focus on emotion rather than logic. Some personality profiles might be more easily persuaded by rational arguments, although vulnerability to these kinds of arguments will also depend on if the attitude we are trying to change (trusting the handler) is based on emotion or cognition. Emotional arguments seem to be more persuasive than rational arguments when the attitude is emotionally-based, although they seem to have a similar effect than rational arguments in cognitive-based attitudes104.
