techniques.name: Approach the source: Direct message
techniques.description: The handler may send a direct message to the source through social media platforms such as Instagram or Twitter, underground forums such as Exploit.in or Breachforums, or through any other similar platform. Independently of the pretext used by the handler, this subtechnique focuses on direct contacts with the source initiated by a handler through an online platform.

techniques.name: Approach the source: Reaction
techniques.description: The handler may react to a comment, post or story of the source on social media. This subtechnique does not refer to commenting or sending written responses to the source, but to the act of “reacting” with some kind of multimedia to what the source posted. For example, if the source posts a story on Instagram, the handler can react with a fire emoji to indicate that they like or feel attracted to that picture, without explicitly sending a message that says “hey, I think you look good in your photo”.

techniques.name: Manipulate feelings of hate
techniques.description: The handler may link existing or newly created feelings of hate towards an object to a tangible manifestation of said emotion to manipulate the source into collaborating. This is done by offering the source a way in which they can express or relieve their hate feelings. In an online HUMINT context, a handler might exploit a source's animosity towards a corrupt government official by amplifying their feelings of hatred and frustration. The handler could leverage the source’s hate towards this official by presenting an opportunity for the source to anonymously contribute information that could expose the official, providing a tangible outlet for the source's emotions.

techniques.name: Manipulate fears
techniques.description: The handler may link existing or newly created feelings of fear towards an object to its reduction to manipulate the source into collaborating. This technique requires the use of positive or negative reinforcement techniques in the tactic UTA0005 - Influence. Moreover, the specific implementation of this technique can be consulted in the technique “UT0512.008 Argument in itself: Fear”. In an online HUMINT context, a handler could manipulate a source by amplifying their fear of a specific cybersecurity threat, such as a data breach. The handler might first spread targeted information about how widespread and devastating this threat could be, creating a heightened sense of fear and urgency in the source. Once the source is sufficiently alarmed, the handler would then offer "solutions" or guidance that appears to mitigate this fear—such as exclusive access to protective measures or insider information on avoiding breaches.

techniques.name: Elevate the source's ego
techniques.description: The handler may elevate the ego of a low self-esteem source or an egocentric source by flattering them in order to make them interact more. This can be done by asking about what the source does best, showing interest in them or praising them into admitting their role in an event, amongst others.

techniques.name: Conduct ego-baiting
techniques.description: The handler may hurt the source's ego to incite them to respond or act in a specific way, usually out of pride or to defend their self-image. This method relies on challenging, questioning, or even undermining someone's capabilities, opinions, or achievements with the goal of eliciting an emotional response. By pricking the individual's ego, the aim is to provoke them into reacting to prove their worth or correct the perception that has been suggested about them. In the cyber domain, particularly where pseudo-anonymity is prevalent, this technique can compel targets to inadvertently disclose information about themselves in an effort to validate their claims or actions. Examples of effective triggers might include dismissing a claim as impossible or suggesting that the target lacks the necessary knowledge or skill to accomplish what they claim, prompting them to reveal more to prove their competence.

techniques.name: Establish a dominance status
techniques.description: The handler establishes a relationship in which it is clear for the source that the one who will dominate the interactions and relationship is the handler. This may be done by adopting a position of power or authority over the source (boss, handler, expert in a relevant matter, higher rank, etc.). This technique is more effective with certain personality profiles, such as low psychoticism individuals, though it should be conducted with great care when dealing with high psychoticism sources126.

techniques.name: Fake a submissive status
techniques.description: The handler establishes a relationship in which the source thinks that they are one who dominates the interactions and relationship. However, the handler must always remain in control, even if the source does not know. This technique is more effective with certain personality profiles, such as high psychoticism individuals127.

techniques.name: Establish an equal status
techniques.description:
The handler establishes a relationship in which the source thinks both parts have an equal status. For example, this can be done by adopting a persona of the same rank or group as the source. Establishing a relationship where the source perceives both parties as having equal status can be highly effective, especially in contexts where trust and mutual respect are crucial for information exchange. This technique is useful when the handler aims to gain the source's confidence and access to sensitive information or insider knowledge without raising suspicion. For example, if a handler is targeting a mid-level employee at a tech company for intelligence on cybersecurity vulnerabilities or internal protocols, they might adopt a persona of a colleague Inteligencia de Fuentes Humanas’. psicológicas en Inteligencia de Fuentes Humanas’, Reports de inteligencia económica y relaciones internacionales, no. 12 (Vol.) (2023): 1.

or peer within the same professional field or rank. By aligning themselves as an equal, the handler can more easily blend into the source's professional environment, engage in candid discussions, and gather insights without the source feeling pressured or threatened. This approach helps in reducing the source’s defensiveness and makes them more willing to share valuable information, as they perceive the handler as a confidant or a trusted partner rather than an authoritative figure or an outsider.

techniques.name: Seduce the source
techniques.description: The handler tries to seduce the source in order to lower their defenses and make them cooperate either knowingly or unknowingly. This subtechnique is dangerous due to the possibility of the handler developing feelings for the source, which should be prevented at all costs. It must be noted that, in order to correctly seduce a source, they should be previously screened because the approach must be tailored in detail.

techniques.name: Manipulate feelings of love
techniques.description: The handler may direct an already existing source’s feeling of love unrelated to the handler, whether romantic, friendship, love for family or others, to manipulate them into collaborating. This technique works best when combined with the use of positive or negative reinforcement techniques.

techniques.name: Befriend the source
techniques.description: The handler may try to develop a friendship-kind of relationship with the source in order for them to collaborate and reveal information. The handler should not get personally involved with the source and remain emotionally neutral at all costs, even when displaying friendshiplike interactions. This technique can be used as a previous step to UT061 - Seduce the source.

techniques.name: Establish a mutual benefit relationship
techniques.description: The handler may establish a relationship based on mutual benefit. This is especially useful when the source knows they are dealing with an intelligence handler. In this case, it is not feelings that are the focus of the handler's control over the source, but rather a mutual understanding of the usefulness of the relationship for both parts.

techniques.name: Inhibit safe relationship network
techniques.description: The handler may actively work to inhibit the source's ability to establish or maintain a safe relationship network outside of the handler's influence. This can be achieved by discouraging the source from confiding in or seeking support from individuals or groups that could potentially undermine the handler's control over the source. It may involve subtle manipulation tactics aimed at eroding trust in external relationships or creating dependency on the handler for emotional or logistical support. In contexts such as insider threat investigations or intelligence gathering within hostile environments, the handler might want to ensure that the source does not leak information or seek advice from external parties that could compromise the operation. By subtly undermining the source's trust in their existing network—such as colleagues, friends, or online communities —the handler can inhibit the source's ability to form or maintain supportive relationships outside of the handler's influence. This isolation makes the source more reliant on the handler for emotional and logistical support, thereby increasing the handler's control over the source and ensuring a steady flow of valuable information without external interference.

techniques.name: Subvert against own network
techniques.description: The handler may subvert the source's own network, turning individuals within it into unwitting assets or informants for the handler's objectives. This can be accomplished by exploiting vulnerabilities or weaknesses within the source's network, such as leveraging personal relationships, compromising confidential information or creating dissent or conflict among members. By exerting influence over the source's network, the handler can further solidify their control over the source and the collection process.

techniques.name: Gaslight
techniques.description: The handler may gaslight the source in order to distort their judgment by manipulating them psychologically to distort their perception of reality and undermine their trust in their own memory, judgment and sanity. This may difficult the source's defenses and make them emotionally controllable by the handler. It must be noted that the usage of this technique must be subtle, while the potential adverse effects on rapport must be considered prior to using it.

techniques.name: Gaslight: Deny
techniques.description: The handler may deny events or situations that the source remembers accurately, insisting that they never happened or were misconstrued. By repeatedly denying the validity of the source's memories, the handler aims to sow doubt and undermine the source's confidence in their own recollections, making them more vulnerable to subsequent manipulation.

techniques.name: Gaslight: Minimize
techniques.description: The handler may downplay the significance of the source's events or experiences, making them seem trivial or insignificant. Through minimizing the importance of the source's memories, the handler seeks to diminish their sense of reality and erode their trust in their own perceptions, making them more vulnerable to subsequent manipulation.

techniques.name: Gaslight: Divert blame
techniques.description: The handler may shift responsibility for past actions or events onto others, including the source, deflecting accountability away from themselves or their organization. By attributing blame to external factors or individuals, the handler seeks to distort the source's understanding of who is truly responsible, creating confusion and weakening their trust in their own judgment, making them more vulnerable to subsequent manipulation.

techniques.name: Gaslight: Create confusion by introducing contradictory information
techniques.description: The handler may introduce contradictory information or evidence to challenge the source's version of events, causing confusion and undermining their confidence in their own memory. By presenting conflicting narratives, the handler aims to distort the source's perception of reality and weaken their defenses, making them more vulnerable to subsequent manipulation.

techniques.name: Gaslight: Isolate
techniques.description: The handler may isolate the source from sources of external validation or support, such as friends, family, or other trusted individuals. By limiting the source's access to alternative perspectives or reassurance, the handler aims to foster dependence and increase their susceptibility to manipulation.

techniques.name: Coerce
techniques.description: The handler may resort to coercion tactics to maintain control over the relationship with the source. Coercion involves the use of threats or intimidation to compel the source to comply with the handler's directives. This technique aims to instill fear or apprehension in the source, thereby influencing their behavior and cooperation. It must be noted that, although effective in some situations, this technique has negative consequences in long-term relationships, and should therefore be carefully applied to continuous and formal contacts, as it could undermine the trust in the handler. Moreover, it should be used with care in elicitation operations, as the source can not know they are dealing, collaborating with or being coerced by an intelligence agency.

techniques.name: Coerce: Threat to expose information
techniques.description: The handler may use the threat of exposing sensitive or compromising information about the source to coerce them into cooperation. This could include threatening to reveal personal secrets, past indiscretions, or illegal activities that could damage the source's reputation or livelihood. By leveraging the fear of exposure, the handler seeks to manipulate the source into complying with their demands and divulging valuable intelligence.

techniques.name: Coerce: Threat to expose collaboration
techniques.description: Similarly, the handler may employ the threat of revealing the source's collaboration with intelligence operatives to coerce their continued cooperation. This tactic capitalizes on the source's desire to maintain secrecy and avoid repercussions for their involvement in clandestine activities. The handler may insinuate or directly threaten to disclose the source's role as an informant or asset, thereby compelling them to adhere to the handler's instructions and provide ongoing intelligence support.

techniques.name: Test the source
techniques.description: The handler may give false information to the source in order to check if the person is trustworthy or if they give the information to someone else. The difference between this technique and the Impact technique “VT0707 - Deceive the adversary” is that, in UT0619, the handler's goal is to check if their control over the source is intact and if they can continue working with them, whereas the goal of VT0707 is to feed false information to an adversary via the source.

techniques.name: Establish security words
techniques.description: The handler may agree with the source on the use of security words or phrases easily identifiable by both parties so as to let the other know that he/she is not in a condition to speak freely. This can be used in either messaging apps or voice calls indistinctly. Although several words for different situations can be agreed, it is recommended to assess to what extent the source will be capable of remembering the keys when needed, specially, considering that these words will not be used on a regular basis in standard interactions but on situations of stress or risk for the source.

techniques.name: Use trusted third party
techniques.description: The handler may leverage the influence of a trusted third party to exert pressure on the source. This tactic involves identifying individuals who hold sway over the source, whether through familial, professional or personal connections. By subtly involving these third parties in the interaction, the handler can indirectly influence the source's behavior and decisions. This technique capitalizes on the source's existing relationships and social dynamics to reinforce compliance with the handler's objectives.

techniques.name: Generate a fog bank
techniques.description:
The handler may stop an argument with the source by creating fog banks. This technique involves telling the other person that they are right, which makes them stop arguing for a moment, allowing the handler to reorient the conversation.

The handler is trying to obtain the required information or access from the source in order to fulfill the operation's requirements. Impact techniques regarding the collection of information must not be confused with the information gathering of the screening phase, the first one being the collection of specific information required by the agency while the second refers to finding out general source areas of knowledge.

techniques.name: Identify other reachable targets
techniques.description: The handler may target the source as a way of identifying new possible sources that can be approached in the future. This involves analyzing the source’s professional and personal relationships, affiliations, and frequent interactions to map a network of individuals who might possess valuable information or influence. The handler looks for strategic entry points within this network, planning subsequent approaches based on the identified connections and their relevance to the operation’s goals.

techniques.name: Obtain access to other source
techniques.description: The handler may target the source as a way of gaining access to a new source using connections to additional valuable sources within the same network or organization. This technique involves leveraging the initial source’s trust and access to introduce or recommend the handler (or another operative) to new contacts. This may occur through direct introductions, gaining entry to meetings, or being included in communications channels where the handler can establish relationships with these new sources.

techniques.name: Expose operational information
techniques.description: The handler may target specific operational details known to the source that are pertinent to their day-to-day duties and responsibilities within their role, either voluntarily or not. This might include workflows, tactical procedures, or immediate operational data that the source encounters through their regular activities.

techniques.name: Expose organizational information
techniques.description: The handler may aim to uncover broader strategic information about the source’s organization, such as long-term plans, organizational hierarchies, or hidden alliances. This information often requires a deeper level of access or understanding, transcending mere operational data to include insights that can impact the strategic positioning of the organization.

techniques.name: Expose access information
techniques.description: The handler may aim to uncover specific details about the access levels or credentials that the source possesses within their network or organization. This technique focuses on obtaining information that can lead to gaining unauthorized access to restricted areas, systems, or data, crucial for the advancement of the operation. This might involve learning about security protocols, passwords, or physical access controls that the source has access to.

techniques.name: Expose access information: Security protocols
techniques.description: The handler may gather detailed information about the security protocols in place within the source’s organization. This includes information on cybersecurity measures, physical security procedures, and any emergency response protocols. Understanding these protocols allows the handler to plan how to circumvent security measures discreetly and safely, ensuring operational success while minimizing risks to the source and the operation.

techniques.name: Expose access information: Physical access
techniques.description: The handler may seek to obtain detailed information about the source's physical access privileges within their organization, such as security badges, keys, or access codes, which can facilitate unauthorized physical entry into secure locations.

techniques.name: Expose access information: Virtual access
techniques.description: The handler may target information related to the source’s virtual access privileges, such as passwords, user IDs, and other credentials that allow for unauthorized access to digital systems, networks, or sensitive data repositories.

techniques.name: Expose personal information
techniques.description: The handler may aim to use the source to expose personal information to gain deeper psychological leverage or compliance. This includes personal details about the source (such as personal beliefs, family matters, financial information, or health issues amongst others) which may have been identified as an intelligence requirement. The main difference with any control technique involving personal information is that the ultimate goal of UT0706 - Expose personal information is the exposure of the information itself.

techniques.name: Expose personal information: Source
techniques.description: The handler may obtain personal information directly related to the source to influence or control their actions.

techniques.name: Expose personal information: Third party
techniques.description: The handler may obtain personal information about individuals close to the source, such as family members, colleagues, or friends, to exert pressure or influence over the source.

techniques.name: Deceive the adversary
techniques.description:
The handler may have the goal of providing false information to the source in order to deceive an adversary, aiming to disrupt their judgment about a specific issue and, therefore, their decision making process. The difference between the technique in Control “UT0619 - Test the source” and this technique is that, in UT0619, the handler's goal is to check if their control over the source is intact and if they can continue working with them, whereas the goal of UT0707 is to feed false information to an adversary via the source.

The handler is trying to report all the information they obtained during the encounter or interaction with a source, archiving it correctly. Although some of the information recovered from human interactions is assumed to remain tacit, online handling has the advantage of the information already being digitized to some extent. This is remarkable since while the recording of a conversation in the real world can be considered an intrusive and aggressive behavior, in online messaging apps it is assumed the “feature” of textual conversations are being logged by both sides. In this regard, it is paramount to pay attention and keep track of the way in which the information is collected and stored. In many cases, this tactic will (and should) coexist with preexisting approaches of conventional real-life human handling.

techniques.name: Report source's personal information
techniques.description: The handler documents comprehensively and analyzes all relevant information about the source. This process is essential for creating detailed profiles that can be referenced in ongoing and future operations and anticipating behaviors of the handler.

techniques.name: Report source's personal information: Biographical information
techniques.description: The handler compiles and records all available biographical data on the source, including basic identifiers such as name, age, birthplace, education, employment status, marital status, and family context amongst others.

techniques.name: Report source's personal information: Personality profile
techniques.description: The handler documents the personality features of the source, behavioral trends, psychological profiles, and any other predictable behavior that could be used in the context of the relationship. In order to be able to effectively understand the interactions, it is recommended to use a tested psychological model such as the Eysenck’s model or any other well-known and standardized model within the organization so as to ease the comprehension and transference of information between handlers if needed.

techniques.name: Report source's personal information: Motivations and interests
techniques.description: The handler assesses and documents the source's motivations, interests, and goals. This information should be attached to the profile to deeply understand the source's network, influences, and potential biases while anticipating any potential conflict of interests.

techniques.name: Report source's personal information: Affiliations
techniques.description: The handler documents any affiliations the source may have within political spheres, trade unions, activist organizations, religious groups, and other significant social or cultural entities. This information should be attached to the profile to be taken into account to deeply understand the source's network, influences, and potential biases while anticipating any potential conflict of interests.

techniques.name: Report encounter or interaction
techniques.description: The handler may write down the key points of each encounter or interaction with the source, providing a detailed chronological list of interactions. Although this may not be the case of all online interactions (which can be logged easily when using online applications), it can be the case in certain interactions such as, for example, when the source is being assigned a specific task or duty. This process can include documenting who initiated the meeting and the notice period given before the encounter, which shows the urgency and formal nature of the request (e. g., official, standard. or urgent). The method of requesting the meeting should be noted alongside any persistence shown in maintaining the engagement, indicating the importance or sensitivity of the discussion. Additionally, the handler may register the selection of communication channel used and who reaches the meeting point first, as well as any preliminary scouting done considered necessary to secure either the meeting location or the digital environment by both sides, handler and source. Observations about the source’s attitude, especially deviations from their baseline behavior, should be carefully noted, specifically when assigning new tasks or bringing information considered to be sensitive for the source, providing insights into their current psychological state and potential pressures or influences if identified.

techniques.name: Tacyt collection of evidence
techniques.description: The handler may trust their memory and experience to register the contacts later on. Information is stored on a personal basis and shared in a conventional unstructured way.

techniques.name: Manual written collection of evidence
techniques.description: The handler may log the contacts manually, either on paper or in any other digital format. Information is stored on a personal basis and shared as conventional physical or digital files or content.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps
techniques.description: The handler may track the raw conversation using native application databases, keeping the record in the application itself for further check with no additional processing. The information is stored basically as a log with basic elements of interaction including participants, dates, hours and other metadata.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps: Text
techniques.description: The handler may use native applications database to store text.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps: Audio
techniques.description: The handler may use native applications database to store audio messages.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps: Images
techniques.description: The handler may use native applications database to store images.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps: Images
techniques.description: The handler may use native applications database to store video content.

techniques.name: Log conversation in native apps: Files
techniques.description: The handler may use native applications database to store files.

techniques.name: Database log storage
techniques.description: The handler may store the information as part of a normalized or standardized database. This information can include, but it is not limited to, personality profile, relationships, encounters, preferences, files, photographs, and any other kind of information which might be considered useful.

techniques.name: Non-standard multimedia recording
techniques.description: The handler may make efforts to register content which is not expected to be stored by default in the tools used employing third party applications. This can involve the storage of photographs which are shared only once by using third party applications to grab the information or recording conversations or messages without explicitly notifying the target.

techniques.name: Manual text transcription
techniques.description: The handler may take on his own the transcription role to effectively register the content of a conversation previously recorded. Additional comments and notes can be added to the transcription to register emotions, feelings or side thoughts that might not be easy to identify in both the raw conversation or an automated transcription. Note that this effort might also take place after an automated transcription to highlight elements of the conversation which could not be processed properly due to environmental issues.

techniques.name: Automated text transcription
techniques.description: The handler may use automated transcription tools to register the content of a recorded audio or video conversation.

techniques.name: Automated text transcription: Live transcription
techniques.description: The handler may use live text transcription tools in the course of the conversation.

techniques.name: Automated text transcription: Post-mortem transcription
techniques.description: The handler may use automated text transcription tools which are not launched live. In this subtechnique, the action takes place after the contact using local or remote tools.

techniques.name: Translate content
techniques.description: The handler may translate content for further debriefing and sharing with other analysts.

techniques.name: Translate content: Self-translation
techniques.description: The handler may perform the translation on their own.

techniques.name: Translate content: Automated translation
techniques.description: The handler may use third party services to perform the translation.

techniques.name: Translate content: Third party translation
techniques.description: The handler may request to a third party or team to perform the translation.

techniques.name: Automated face recognition
techniques.description: The handler may use face recognition tools to identify people on virtual or physical content shared by the source. This action involves the initial use of face detection tools to identify faces of people appearing in the content first and a second face recognition phase against a set of known faces.

techniques.name: Automated voice recognition
techniques.description: The handler may use voice recognition tools to identify people on virtual or physical content shared by the source. This action may involve the use of voice modeling tools to identify relevant signatures in the recorded voice and a second voice recognition phase against a set of known voices.

techniques.name: Automated text recognition
techniques.description: The handler may use text recognition tools to identify textual content from images or other media shared by the author that involves photographs of the content instead of raw text.

techniques.name: Automated object recognition
techniques.description: The handler may use object recognition tools to identify objects and elements present in the contents shared. This may involve the use of models trained to identify vehicles, animals, people, or other objects, tagging the elements.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy
techniques.description: The handler may conduct continuous monitoring of the situation and relationship with the source in order to evaluate the progress of the operation, and whether any of its components need to be adapted or reassessed.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: Other HUMINT
techniques.description: The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with other Human Intelligence (HUMINT) sources.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: OSINT
techniques.description: The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) sources.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: SIGINT
techniques.description: The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with Signals Intelligence (SIGINT) sources.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: GEOINT
techniques.description: The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with Geospatial Intelligence (GEOINT) sources.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: IMINT
techniques.description: The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with Image Intelligence (IMINT) sources.

techniques.name: Assess content’s accuracy: SOCINT
techniques.description:
The handler may verify the accuracy of the information with Socialmedia intelligence (SOCINT) sources.

The handler is trying to exit an interaction or to close the relationship with the source. This process requires careful planning to ensure the termination of contact does not provoke undesirable reactions or leave loose ends that could compromise the operation or the safety of either the handler or source.

techniques.name: Exit contact
techniques.description: The handler may end a single contact session or interaction with clear closure, not wanting to close the door open for future interactions. This may include summarizing what was discussed, thanking the source for their cooperation, and subtly indicating when they might next be in touch. The difference with “UT0902 - Foresee future contacts'' is that the contact ends with no specific additional tasks for the source. Therefore, this technique can be used with both onetime contacts and continuous and formal contacts, whereas “UT0902 - Foresee future contacts” can not be used with one-time contacts, as in this kind of contacts the sources do not get tasked.

techniques.name: Exit contact: Reinforce future contacts
techniques.description: The handler may ensure that while the current interaction concludes without further immediate obligations, the source remains engaged and open to future contact. This technique involves reinforcing the importance of the relationship and expressing appreciation for the source's past cooperation. The handler may subtly emphasize the ongoing value the source could provide, hinting at future discussions without setting specific expectations, or maybe even hinting at possible future reinforcements. This approach helps maintain a positive rapport and keeps the communication channels open for re-engagement at a later date. This subtechnique should be planned and executed while considering the personality and motivational profile of the source, as individual's with a higher extraversion and neuroticism tend to be more vulnerable to rewards. Moreover, the type of reward, whether anticipated or given at the time of the exit, should also be tailored to the person. Therefore, this technique works best when combined with the behavioral techniques in UTA05 - Influence.

techniques.name: Exit contact: Anticipate a punishment for withdrawing the relationship
techniques.description: The handler may subtly warn or imply that disengagement or non-cooperation from the source could have negative repercussions. This technique is used cautiously, primarily as a psychological strategy to ensure compliance and discourage the source from severing ties or becoming uncooperative. The handler may hint at potential difficulties the source could face if they withdraw from the relationship, framing the ongoing cooperation as beneficial or the lesser of two evils. This is done subtly to maintain a cooperative stance rather than resorting to overt threats, which could damage the relationship. This subtechnique should be planned and executed while considering the personality and motivational profile of the source, as individual's with a lower extraversion and neuroticism tend to be more vulnerable to punishments. Moreover, the type of punishment, whether anticipated or given at the time of the exit, should also be tailored to the person. Therefore, this technique works best when combined with the behavioral techniques in UTA05 - Influence. In addition to these considerations, it must be noted that a punishment works best when an alternative behavior to the one that is being punished gets rewarded. In HUMINT cases, this can be applied by both subtly announcing the punishment a certain behavior, like talking to others about the relationship between the handler and the source, and rewarding an alternative, like explaining how beneficial it would be for the source to continue bringing information to the handler without anyone knowing.

techniques.name: Plan future contacts
techniques.description: The handler may finish a contact or interaction with a source by laying the groundwork for future contacts. This might involve scheduling future meetings, suggesting topics for future discussion, or other pretexts that justify continued engagement. This method helps maintain the relationship’s viability and ensures ongoing access to valuable intelligence. The difference with “UT0901 - Exit contact” is that, in this technique, the closure of the contact maintains an agenda or list of scheduled tasks or interests. This technique can only be used with formal contacts because these are defined as the only sources with whom future encounters can be planned. Moreover, if used subtly, this technique can be also used with continuous contacts in elicitation operations, as long as the source does not know who they are collaborating with.

techniques.name: Openly terminate relationship
techniques.description: The handler may formally end the relationship with the source. This involves a definitive conclusion to their interactions, often needed by the completion of the source’s usefulness or potential security risks. The termination process should be handled with care to ensure that the source does not feel betrayed, which could lead to retaliation, feelings of revenge, or potential leaks.

techniques.name: Change handler
techniques.description: The handler may introduce the source to another handler. This technique is used to transition the source smoothly from one handler to another without disrupting the flow of information or, even, alerting the source to the change. In this technique, the handler knows they are interacting with an intelligence agency. Note that any handler selection techniques seen in previous sections apply here.

techniques.name: Change persona
techniques.description: The handler may introduce the source to another fake virtual persona operated by another handler. This technique is used to transition the source smoothly from one handler to another without disrupting the flow of information or, even, alerting the source to the change. In this technique, the source does not know they are interacting with an intelligence agency. Note that any handler selection techniques seen in previous sections apply here.

techniques.name: Ghosting
techniques.description: The handler may stop answering the source’s messages either progressively or abruptly to reduce or cut contact, ensuring a non-confrontational withdrawal from communication. Note that this may have emotional implications for the source if unexplained, but it can be a tool to face situations in which the handler or the source may be overinvolved.

techniques.name: Ghosting: Permanent
techniques.description: The handler may cease all communication with the source permanently, without prior notice or subsequent explanation. This method is used when no further information is required from the source, or if continuing the relationship poses a greater risk that any expected undesirable reaction of the source to such a termination.

techniques.name: Ghosting: Temporal
techniques.description: The handler may temporarily stop communication with the source, often as a strategic move to evaluate the source’s reaction, to de-escalate a situation or to generate addiction. The intention is to resume contact at a later date under more favorable conditions.

techniques.name: Ghosting: Progressive
techniques.description: The handler may gradually reduce the frequency of communication with the source, effectively phasing out the relationship over time. This approach can help to reduce the impact of the cessation and is often used to smartly disengage with minimal suspicion or without arousing suspicion at all.

techniques.name: Alter memory
techniques.description:
The handler employs techniques designed to alter or reshape the source’s memories of their interactions or the nature of their relationship. This could involve suggesting alternative narratives or contexts for events that occurred, effectively creating a less accurate but potentially less harmful memory of their involvement. This is particularly useful in sensitive operations where the source’s accurate recall of events could pose a risk.

The handler is trying to detect indicators of suspicious activity of the source, as well as to respond to said indicators in such a way that protects them and the overall operation. The main difference of this tactic when compared to the previous nine is that, while the rest are proactive, UTA10 is reactive, meaning that it includes possible ways in which the handler can react to unexpected behaviors of the source. This tactic includes what in other TTPs frameworks is dubbed mitigations or “blue” actions, as it comprises the defensive actions a handler can take when the source is or seems to be trying to influence, control or obtain anything from the handler.

techniques.name: Detect deception risk
techniques.description: The handler may try to detect the level of deception risk of the source. In order to do this, the handler will initially establish a baseline of the normal behavioral indicators of truthfulness of the source, which will later be used to detect deviations from in that may indicate a higher risk of deception. It must be noted that the detection of one deceit indicator or baseline deviation does not necessarily imply that the source is lying. In order to consider that the deceit risk has increased, multiple indicators (a cluster) must be detected and, even then, it does not mean that the source is necessarily lying since there might be other environmental circumstances that may trigger these indicators such as thinking something through (not necessarily trying to lie), amongst others. The deception detection risk encompasses the following phases: establish baseline, create and enforce a detection plan and detect deviation indicators.

techniques.name: Detect deception risk: Establish baseline
techniques.description:
The handler may establish the source's (truthfulness) baseline; that is, how they normally act when telling the truth. Indicators must be obtained and registered during every interaction with the source. It must be noted that baselines often are related with the personality profile of the source, hence the need to consider said profile when understanding a baseline, especially since high psychoticism sources find it easier to lie and therefore it is harder to detect them128 . Indicators that would be considered in order to understand the baseline include the following:

Response time

Verbal indicators

Body language

Facial expressions

techniques.name: Detect deception risk: Create and enforce a detection plan
techniques.description: The handler may create a deception detection plan, in which the following phases are conducted in order to detect the source's deceit indicators in a controlled context or environment129: 1. Relax the source, do not ask about the topic used to check for indicators and register the behavioral baseline. 2. Ask a control question, that is, a question of which the answer is known, and check for baseline deviations if they lie. 3. Go back to a relaxation period, change topics and ask unrelated questions. 4. Repeat phases 2 and 3 as long as it is needed. 5. Close the interaction as required.

techniques.name: Detect deception risk: Detect deviation indicators
techniques.description:
The handler may detect indicators of baseline deviation that may imply a higher risk of deceit. As it has been mentioned before, the detection of one deceit indicator or baseline deviation does not necessarily imply that the source is lying. In order to consider that the deceit risk has increased, multiple indicators must be detected and, even then, it does not mean that the source is necessarily lying. Deceit indicators may not be easily detected in chat conversations, which are a context in which the handler should focus on verbal and response time indicators. However, technical deception detection tools may be used if the handler has access to the camera of the source, or the source themselves contact by video. If a recording of the conversation can be registered, the information might be post-processed after the meeting for a more detailed postmortem analysis of the encounter. Similarly to the baseline deviation detection that takes place in physical interactions, caused by the neurological stress that lying brings130, some indicators that may also be useful in the virtual domain may include but are not limited to:

A change in response time

Pupil dilation or contraction

Eye contact variation

Eye openness variation

Cerebral Cortex (New York, N.Y.: 1991) 16, no. 2 (February 2006): 192–99, https://doi.org/10.1093/cercor/bhi097; Nobuhito Abe et al., ‘Neural Correlates of True Memory, False Memory, and Deception’, Cerebral Cortex (New York, NY) 18, no. 12 (December 2008): 2811–19, https://doi.org/10.1093/cercor/bhn037; Nobuhito Abe et al., ‘Deceiving Others: Distinct Neural Responses of the Prefrontal Cortex and Amygdala in Simple Fabrication and Deception with Social Interactions’, J. Cognitive Neuroscience 19, no. 2 (1 February 2007): 287–95, https://doi.org/10.1162/jocn.2007.19.2.287; Maria Serena Panasiti et al., ‘Thermal Signatures of Voluntary Deception in Ecological Conditions’, Scientific Reports 6 (13 October 2016): 35174, https://doi.org/10.1038/srep35174.

Blinking frequency variation

Contradiction between body language and verbal cues

Body manipulation frequency variation

Drinking more or other indicators of higher mouth dryness

Closed, tense lips

Defensive or tense body language

Spasiform movements

Silence variations

Faking emotions

Exaggeration

Voice volume and tone variations

Longer or shorter explanations

Speech changes

Avoiding questions

The alleged knowledge does not match the source's rank or position

Detail level variation

Self-serving information

Increased sweating

Increased facial redness

Source's appearance, language usage or technical vocabulary does not match their story

Repeating answers with the same wording (as if memorized)

techniques.name: Record
techniques.description: The handler may act normally and show no immediate reaction to suspicious behavior exhibited by the source, instead quietly documenting and archiving the information. This technique allows the handler to maintain the flow of the interaction without alerting the source to any concerns. By saving the information for later analysis, the handler can build a comprehensive picture of the source's behavior over time, supporting more informed decisions in future interactions.

techniques.name: Report immediately
techniques.description:
The handler may immediately report any detected indicators of suspicious activity to their superiors or relevant team members. This quick response ensures that potential threats are communicated without delay, allowing the organization to take timely action to mitigate risks.

Prompt reporting helps maintain the integrity and security of the operation by enabling a coordinated response to emerging threats.

techniques.name: Inquire without confronting
techniques.description: The handler may subtly inquire about the suspicious behavior without directly confronting the source. This approach involves asking open-ended questions or expressing curiosity about the observed actions in a non-threatening manner such as “Sorry. I don't know if I understood you correctly, is this what you mean?” or other similar inquiries. By doing so, the handler can gather additional information and gain insights into the source's motivations while maintaining rapport and avoiding defensive reactions.

techniques.name: Confront
techniques.description: The handler may directly confront the source about their suspicious behavior. This technique involves addressing the issue head-on, expressing concerns, and seeking an explanation from the source. While this approach carries the risk of escalating tensions, it can be effective in clarifying misunderstandings or deterring the source from engaging in further suspicious activities.

techniques.name: Turn the tables
techniques.description: The handler may use the source's suspicious behavior to their advantage by turning the situation around. This could involve leveraging the detected behavior to extract additional information, gain leverage or manipulate the source into revealing more than they intended. This technique requires careful handling to avoid arousing suspicion or damaging the relationship with the source.

techniques.name: Deceive
techniques.description: The handler may employ deception as a defensive measure when indicators suggest that the source might be attempting to manipulate, influence or extract information from the handler. This technique involves presenting false or misleading information to the source, as well as hiding or faking the handler’s emotions and gestures to obscure their true intentions, capabilities or operational details. This tactic must be used judiciously and with careful consideration of potential long-term consequences on the handler-source relationship and the operation's overall success.

techniques.name: Deceive: Hide possible baseline changes in the reaction
techniques.description: The handler may consciously hide any changes in their behavior or demeanor that might indicate recognition of the source's suspicious activity. By maintaining a consistent baseline in their reactions, the handler prevents the source from realizing that their actions have been detected, thus preserving the element of surprise and control over the situation.

techniques.name: Deceive: Fake baseline changes
techniques.description: The handler may deliberately alter their behavior to simulate a change in baseline reactions, creating a false impression for the source. This technique can be used to manipulate the source's perceptions, elicit specific responses or test the source's reactions to perceived changes in the handler's demeanor. It is a strategic way to influence the interaction without revealing true intentions.

techniques.name: Deceive: Lie
techniques.description: The handler may choose to lie in response to the source's suspicious behavior. This deceptive tactic can be used to protect sensitive information, mislead the source, or aid to maintain the control the direction of the conversation. While lying carries ethical and operational risks, it can be a necessary measure in situations where the truth could compromise the operation.

techniques.name: Deceive: Fake emotion or reaction
techniques.description: The handler may feign an emotional response or reaction to the source's behavior. This technique involves displaying a contrived emotional state, such as surprise, concern, or indifference, to influence the source's perceptions and behavior. By controlling their emotional display, the handler can guide the interaction in a desired direction and potentially elicit useful information from the source.

techniques.name: Deny
techniques.description: The handler may outright deny any accusations, implications or suspicious interpretations put forth by the source. This technique involves a firm and straightforward refutation of any claims, maintaining a stance of innocence or ignorance. Denial can be an effective way to deflect suspicion.

techniques.name: Remind
techniques.description: The handler may remind the source of previous agreements, understandings or the potential consequences of their actions. This technique involves subtly or explicitly referencing past conversations, commitments or established norms to coerce the source into compliance or deter them from continuing suspicious behavior. By invoking reminders, the handler reinforces the operational framework and expectations.

techniques.name: Change the situation
techniques.description: The handler may change the physical or contextual setting of the interaction to disrupt the source's suspicious activities. This could involve relocating the meeting, altering the communication medium or introducing new environmental variables. Changing the situation can disorient the source and provide the handler with an opportunity to regain control and reassess the interaction.

techniques.name: Move to safe topic
techniques.description: The handler may strategically redirect the conversation to a safer or less sensitive topic to maintain control over the interaction. This tactic involves avoiding potential triggers or contentious subjects that could disrupt the flow of the conversation or jeopardize the relationship with the source. By steering the discussion towards neutral or non-threatening topics, the handler can mitigate the risk of confrontation, resistance or disclosure of sensitive information. This technique allows the handler to maintain a sense of control and direction in the conversation while minimizing potential disruptions or obstacles.

techniques.name: Leave the interaction
techniques.description: The handler may choose to terminate the interaction abruptly in response to the source's suspicious behavior. This technique involves exiting the conversation or physical location to prevent further exposure or risk. Leaving the interaction can serve as a protective measure, allowing the handler to regroup, reassess the situation and plan a more strategic response.

techniques.name: Conduct counterintelligence checks
techniques.description: The handler may conduct counterintelligence checks once a suspicious indicator has been identified to subsequently mitigate any potential infiltration or influence attempts by adversarial entities. This involves verifying the background, associations and behavior of the source, looking for any anomalies or inconsistencies that might indicate subversive activities. By performing these checks, the handler can detect and respond to potential threats, ensuring that the source remains trustworthy and that the operation remains secure.

techniques.name: Reevaluate technical risks
techniques.description: The handler may review and enhance technical safety measures when it becomes apparent that the source or adversary is more proficient at technology and cyberattacks than initially anticipated. This technique involves conducting a thorough assessment of existing security protocols, such as encrypted communications, secure file storage, and access controls, to identify potential vulnerabilities. The handler will then implement additional or upgraded measures, including advanced encryption practices, multi-factor authentication and sophisticated counter-surveillance techniques, to fortify the operation’s defenses. By proactively adapting to the heightened technical capabilities of the source or adversary, the handler ensures the continued protection of communication channels, data integrity and overall operational security.
